skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate AI videos with the inference.sh CLI using models for text-to-video, image-to-video, lipsync, avatars, upscaling, foley, and media merging.
skill.summary: Create AI video assets through inference.sh model commands.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mostly Markdown command examples and hardcoded documentation or sample media URLs, not hidden executable code. One confirmed high-risk issue remains: the quick start recommends piping a remote installer directly into sh, which creates supply-chain and command-execution risk for users. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential theft, obfuscation, or malicious intent in the reviewed file.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped To Shell
high_findings.description: The quick start instructs users to download an installer from a remote URL and execute it directly with sh. This is dangerous because a compromised endpoint, DNS path, or network response could execute arbitrary code before the user can inspect it.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell command is explicit on the referenced line. The surrounding text presents it as an installation path, so the risk is real even though malicious intent is not proven.

medium_findings.title: Expected Network Submission To Third-Party Video Models
medium_findings.description: The skill sends prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, and video URLs to inference.sh apps and downstream model providers. This is core functionality, but users must understand that media inputs and prompts leave the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly run remote inference apps and pass user-provided media or prompt values. This behavior appears legitimate for the skill category but carries privacy and data handling risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many model names and fenced command examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed context shows Markdown formatting and CLI examples, not embedded Ruby execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens appear inside Markdown tables or fenced bash examples. No Ruby code or dynamic interpreter context is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Mostly Documentation And Placeholders
low_findings.description: Most hardcoded URLs point to project documentation, a preview image, install metadata, or placeholder media inputs. These are expected for a CLI usage guide, except for the separate installer execution concern.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links or sample input placeholders. I did not find evidence that they are used for covert exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Flags Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings at the cited lines do not correspond to an actual weak algorithm use. The only checksum discussion found is a SHA-256 verification note, which is not a weak cryptographic algorithm in this context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive metadata and a table header, not cryptographic code. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or other weak algorithm usage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a remote installer command that immediately executes downloaded content with sh. Marketplace publication should require a safer installation path, such as manual download with checksum verification before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command combines network download and shell execution on the same line. The install note reduces but does not remove the risk.

content.user_title: Create AI Videos with Inference.sh Models
content.value_statement: Teams need repeatable ways to create video assets without switching between many model tools. This skill provides focused inference.sh CLI prompts for text-to-video, image animation, lipsync, upscaling, foley, and media merging.
actual_capabilities: Lists supported inference.sh video model IDs for text-to-video and image-to-video workflows.
Provides ready-to-adapt CLI examples for Veo, Grok Video, Seedance, Wan, OmniHuman, Fabric, and PixVerse.
Shows how to run video utility apps for foley sound, upscaling, and media merging.
Guides users toward related inference.sh skills for avatars, text-to-speech, image generation, and social posting.
Links to inference.sh documentation for running apps and streaming results.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Video generation quality, speed, and cost depend on the selected remote model.
Input media URLs and prompts are processed by external services.
The included installer command needs safer manual verification before marketplace publication.

use_cases.title: Produce Social Video Concepts
use_cases.description: Create short video drafts from written prompts for campaigns, reels, and channel tests.

use_cases.title: Animate Product And Brand Images
use_cases.description: Turn still images into motion clips for product demos, launch pages, or presentation assets.

use_cases.title: Build Avatar And Lipsync Clips
use_cases.description: Generate talking-head or lipsynced clips from portrait and audio inputs for explainers.

prompt_templates.title: Generate A Simple Video
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a short AI video from this idea using the fastest suitable inference.sh video model: [describe the scene].

prompt_templates.title: Animate A Still Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use an image-to-video model to animate this image URL: [image URL]. The movement should be [motion style] and the final clip should fit [use case].

prompt_templates.title: Create A Talking Avatar
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a talking avatar video from this portrait URL and audio URL. Choose the best listed model and explain any required input constraints.

prompt_templates.title: Build A Multi-Step Video Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan and run an inference.sh video workflow that creates a clip, adds foley sound, upscales it, and merges it with these additional clips: [clip list].

output_examples.input: A request to create a forest flyover video from a written prompt.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects a fast Veo model, prepares a CLI run, and returns the generated video result link when inference.sh completes.

output_examples.input: A portrait image and voiceover audio for an explainer clip.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends an avatar or lipsync model, submits the portrait and audio inputs, and reports the resulting talking-head video.

output_examples.input: A silent video that needs sound effects and higher resolution.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs a foley model, then an upscaler, and summarizes the final media outputs for review.

best_practices: Use non-sensitive media and prompts unless your team has approved the external processing path.
Choose the smallest model and duration that can validate the creative direction.
Verify installer files and checksums manually before running any installation command.
anti_patterns: Do not pipe remote installer scripts directly into a shell in production environments.
Do not submit private faces, voices, or customer media without consent and policy review.
Do not assume each model supports the same duration, resolution, audio, or input fields.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create AI-generated videos, animated images, avatar clips, lipsync clips, upscaled videos, foley audio, and merged media outputs.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it run models locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses inference.sh CLI commands that send work to remote inference.sh apps and model providers.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish as submitted?
faq.answer: Not yet. The install command should be changed to a safer verified installation flow before publication.

faq.question: What inputs should users prepare?
faq.answer: Users should prepare clear prompts, public or approved media URLs, target duration, quality needs, and any audio for avatar or lipsync tasks.

faq.question: Can it help with full video workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for generation, foley sound, upscaling, and merging clips through inference.sh apps.
